era_context: The early Iron Age II in the Levant and Eastern Mediterranean. The Neo-Assyrian Empire is consolidating power under Ashurnasirpal II (883–859 BCE), though this year falls slightly before his major campaigns. In Egypt, the 22nd Dynasty (Libyan) rules; the unified pharaonic state is fragmenting. In Greece, the Geometric period is well established; the Archaic period will begin within a century. In China, the Western Zhou dynasty is in decline, approaching the Spring and Autumn period.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records for this period)
Northern Europe (pre-literate societies; archaeological record is primary source)
Mesoamerica (Maya civilization in Classic period, but specific year-by-year events rarely attested)
South Asia (Vedic period; oral traditions not yet fixed in writing)

events.title: Reign of Ashurnasirpal II begins (traditional date)
events.description: Ashurnasirpal II ascends the throne of Assyria, beginning a reign that will last until 859 BCE. He is remembered as one of the empire's greatest military organizers and administrators, though his exact accession date is debated. He will establish Kalhu (Nimrud) as a new capital and conduct extensive campaigns to consolidate Assyrian power across the Levant and Mesopotamia. His reign marks a turning point in Assyrian imperial expansion.

sources.name: Ashurnasirpal II's own inscriptions and annals (clay tablets and palace reliefs from Kalhu/Nimrud)
Synchronistic History and Babylonian Chronicle fragments

events.certainty_note: Ashurnasirpal II's reign is well-attested in his own inscriptions. However, the exact year of accession varies slightly in modern scholarship (some sources place it at 883 BCE, others at 884 BCE). The traditional date of 883 BCE is widely accepted, making 939 BCE approximately 56 years before his reign. This entry may be misdated; verification against Assyrian king lists is required.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Ashurnasirpal II's accession is itself uncertain within a 1–2 year range. No specific events are reliably attested for the year 939 BCE itself in available sources. The broader era is well-documented through Assyrian royal inscriptions, but year-by-year precision for this period is limited to major campaigns and political transitions, which are not clearly assigned to 939 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 939 BCE is extremely thin. Most surviving documentation from this era comes from Assyrian royal inscriptions, which are propagandistic and focus on military campaigns and royal achievements. Egyptian records from the 22nd Dynasty are fragmentary. Greek sources for this period are mythological or much later. Chinese sources (Shiji) provide dynastic chronology but lack year-specific detail for this date. The absence of evidence for 939 BCE specifically should not be interpreted as absence of historical activity—rather, it reflects the sparse survival of written records from this period.

graph_edges.note: Ashurnasirpal II's reign (883–859 BCE) encompasses major military campaigns and administrative reforms; 939 BCE predates his documented reign.
